title: Enterprise and Regulatory Reform Act 2013
chapters: 103 sections + 22 Schedules. A broad Coalition Government Act covering competition, regulation, and employment. The EMPLOYMENT provisions (Part 2, ss.7-25) are among the most practically impactful in everyday HR management. Five key pillars: (1) PROTECTED CONVERSATIONS — s.111A ERA 1996 enabling confidential exit discussions; (2) MANDATORY ACAS EARLY CONCILIATION — no tribunal claim without first contacting ACAS; (3) FINANCIAL PENALTIES for employers with aggravating features; (4) WHISTLEBLOWING REFORM — public interest test; (5) H&S CIVIL LIABILITY — removal of employee right to sue for breach of H&S statutory duty. The ERA 2025 builds on pillars 1-4 while the controversial pillar 5 remains unchanged.

parliamentaryOrigin.bill: Enterprise and Regulatory Reform Bill [Government Bill]
parliamentaryOrigin.introducedBy: Government Bill — Conservative-Liberal Democrat Coalition Government, Secretary of State for Business, Innovation and Skills Vince Cable
parliamentaryOrigin.commencementDate: Various — phased from June 2013 to April 2014
parliamentaryOrigin.commencementNotes: Whistleblowing and political opinions: 25 June 2013. Protected conversations: 29 July 2013. ACAS early conciliation: 6 April 2014. Financial penalties: 6 April 2014. H&S civil liability: 1 October 2013.

significantAmendments.instrument: Employment Rights Act 2025 — enhancing ERRA 2013 provisions
significantAmendments.effect: The ERA 2025 BUILDS ON the ERRA 2013 framework: (1) ACAS early conciliation extended to 12 weeks (December 2025). (2) Tribunal time limits extended from 3 to 6 months (October 2026) — giving more time before and after ACAS involvement. (3) Compensatory award cap being REMOVED entirely (January 2027) — superseding the ERRA 2013 cap formula. (4) Financial penalty increased. (5) Whistleblowing: sexual harassment becomes a qualifying disclosure (April 2026). The s.111A protected conversations framework is UNCHANGED by ERA 2025.

leadingCases.name: Faithorn Farrell Timms LLP v Bailey
leadingCases.principle: Under s.111A, NEITHER the details NOR the existence of pre-termination negotiations can be relied upon by a claimant in an ordinary unfair dismissal claim. The protection under s.111A and the without prejudice rule operate INDEPENDENTLY. There is no statutory provision permitting waiver of s.111A protection.
leadingCases.heldText: Communications that concern a pre-termination negotiation fall within s.111A regardless of their form — including internal emails and notes.
leadingCases.significance: Critical for employers: s.111A provides a broad protection that covers the existence, content, and surrounding communications of pre-termination negotiations. But the protection ONLY applies to ordinary unfair dismissal claims — NOT discrimination, whistleblowing, or automatic unfair dismissal.

leadingCases.name: Gallagher v McKinnon's Auto and Tyres Ltd
leadingCases.principle: It is NOT improper behaviour to explain that a formal process (e.g. redundancy consultation) would begin if the settlement offer is not accepted — provided this is not framed as a threat of inevitable dismissal. Giving 48 hours to consider a verbal offer is not necessarily improper. The ACAS Code on settlement agreements should be followed but is not rigid.
leadingCases.heldText: There was no impropriety in explaining the alternative process or in setting a 48-hour response window for a verbal offer.
leadingCases.significance: Provides practical guidance for employers on the boundaries of protected conversations. The test for impropriety is fact-sensitive but the bar is not set low — robust negotiation is permitted, but undue pressure is not.

leadingCases.name: Chesterton Global Ltd v Nurmohamed
leadingCases.principle: The 'public interest' test for whistleblowing (introduced by ERRA 2013) does not require the disclosure to be of interest to the public at large. A disclosure can be in the public interest even if it primarily affects the worker making it, provided it has a wider significance — for example, if other workers are also affected by the same breach.
leadingCases.heldText: A disclosure about the employer manipulating commission calculations affecting approximately 100 senior managers was in the public interest, even though the claimant was personally affected.
leadingCases.significance: The leading authority on the ERRA 2013 public interest test. Establishes that the test has a low threshold — the worker need only have a REASONABLE BELIEF that the disclosure is in the public interest. A breach affecting a group of workers will usually satisfy the test.

parts.title: Part 1 — Protected Conversations: The Employer's Most Powerful Tool for Managed Exits

sections.title: Pre-termination negotiations — how to have the conversation, when protection applies, and when it doesn't
sections.summary: Section 111A enables employers and employees to have CONFIDENTIAL discussions about ending employment on agreed terms, even where NO DISPUTE currently exists. The discussions are inadmissible in ordinary unfair dismissal proceedings. This is arguably the most practically useful provision in everyday employment management — it allows employers to propose an exit package honestly and openly without the risk of the conversation being used against them. But it has CRITICAL LIMITATIONS that every employer must understand.
sections.text:
WHAT s.111A DOES:

Before s.111A (pre-2013): An employer who wanted to discuss an exit with an employee had to rely on the common law 'without prejudice' rule. But the without prejudice rule only applies where a DISPUTE already exists. If the employer approaches an employee out of the blue and says 'we'd like to discuss you leaving' — with no prior dispute — the conversation is NOT protected. The employee could use it as evidence in a tribunal claim.

After s.111A (from 29 July 2013): The employer can initiate settlement discussions even where NO DISPUTE exists. The conversation is inadmissible in ordinary unfair dismissal proceedings provided there is no 'improper behaviour'.

THE PRACTICAL SCENARIO — HOW THIS WORKS:

An HR manager has an employee whose performance has been declining, or whose role is becoming redundant, or who simply doesn't fit the team culture. Rather than embarking on a lengthy capability process, redundancy consultation, or disciplinary procedure, the employer can:

(1) Invite the employee to a private meeting.
(2) Explain that the employer would like to have an 'off-the-record' discussion about the employee's future, covered by s.111A.
(3) Propose an exit package: an enhanced payment, a reference, an agreed leaving date.
(4) Give the employee time to consider (the ACAS Code suggests at least 10 calendar days for a written offer).
(5) If the employee accepts, the parties enter into a settlement agreement (with independent legal advice for the employee, paid for by the employer).
(6) If the employee declines, the employer can proceed with the appropriate formal process (capability, redundancy, etc.) — and the protected conversation CANNOT be used as evidence in any subsequent unfair dismissal claim.

WHAT IS PROTECTED:
(a) The CONTENT of the discussions — what was said, offered, or proposed.
(b) The EXISTENCE of the discussions — even the fact that they took place is inadmissible (Faithorn Farrell Timms v Bailey).
(c) SURROUNDING COMMUNICATIONS — internal emails, notes, and documents relating to the negotiations.

WHAT IS NOT PROTECTED — THE CRITICAL LIMITATIONS:

⚠️ s.111A ONLY protects against ORDINARY UNFAIR DISMISSAL claims (ERA s.111). It does NOT protect against:
(a) DISCRIMINATION claims (EqA 2010) — if the discussion reveals discriminatory motivation (e.g., 'we think you should leave because you're getting older'), the employee CAN use the conversation in a discrimination claim.
(b) AUTOMATIC UNFAIR DISMISSAL claims — whistleblowing, pregnancy/maternity, trade union activity.
(c) CONSTRUCTIVE DISMISSAL — if the manner of the conversation is so unreasonable that it amounts to a fundamental breach of the implied term of trust and confidence.
(d) DETRIMENT claims — the conversation cannot be used to subject the employee to detriment.

⚠️ IMPROPER BEHAVIOUR destroys the protection:
The ACAS Code on Settlement Agreements identifies examples of improper behaviour:
(a) Harassment, bullying, or intimidation.
(b) Putting undue pressure on the employee to accept (e.g., 'accept this or you're fired').
(c) Not giving the employee reasonable time to consider.
(d) Not telling the employee they can be accompanied (although there is no legal right to be accompanied at a s.111A meeting, it is good practice).
(e) Discrimination in the discussion.

BEST PRACTICE FOR THE EMPLOYER:
(1) Mark all correspondence 'Without Prejudice and/or Covered by s.111A ERA 1996'.
(2) Explain at the start of the meeting that the discussion is protected.
(3) Do NOT put undue pressure — present the offer as an option, not an ultimatum.
(4) Allow at least 10 calendar days for the employee to consider a written offer.
(5) Offer to pay the employee's legal costs for advice on the settlement agreement (typically £500 + VAT).
(6) If the employee declines, STOP the protected conversation and proceed with the formal process.
(7) Keep notes of the protected conversation SEPARATE from open HR files.

BEST PRACTICE FOR THE EMPLOYEE:
(1) You are NOT obliged to engage in a protected conversation. You can decline.
(2) If you do engage, take notes. Ask for the offer in writing.
(3) Take independent legal advice before signing any settlement agreement.
(4) If the employer behaves improperly — pressuring, threatening, discriminating — the protection may be lost and the conversation may be admissible.
(5) Remember: s.111A only covers unfair dismissal. If you have a discrimination claim, the conversation IS admissible in those proceedings.
sections.keyPrinciple: s.111A is the most practical tool available to employers for managing exits efficiently, humanely, and with legal certainty. It enables honest conversations about separation without the risk of those conversations being used as evidence. But it is NOT a shield for bad behaviour — improper conduct destroys the protection, and the protection does NOT extend to discrimination or automatic unfair dismissal claims. Eileen must help clients understand both the power and the limits of this tool.
refs: ACAS Code of Practice on Settlement Agreements under s.111A
Faithorn Farrell Timms v Bailey UKEAT/0025/16
Gallagher v McKinnon's Auto and Tyres (2024)
Common law without prejudice rule (parallel protection)

regulatoryGuidance.title: Code of Practice on Settlement Agreements (under s.111A)
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: The ACAS Code sets out best practice for conducting protected conversations, including examples of improper behaviour. Tribunals must take the Code into account.

commonErrors: Believing s.111A protects ALL conversations — it ONLY protects against ordinary unfair dismissal claims. Discrimination, whistleblowing, automatic unfair dismissal, and constructive dismissal claims are NOT covered. If the exit discussion reveals discriminatory motivation, the employee can use it in a discrimination claim
Putting undue pressure on the employee — saying 'accept this or you're fired' destroys the protection. The offer must be presented as an option, with genuine time to consider
Not marking correspondence — all written communications relating to the protected conversation should be marked 'Without Prejudice and/or Covered by s.111A ERA 1996'

Conflating s.111A with the without prejudice rule — they operate INDEPENDENTLY. s.111A applies even where no dispute exists (its unique advantage). The without prejudice rule requires an existing dispute but applies to ALL claim types (its unique advantage). Use BOTH labels where possible

Not giving the employee reasonable time to consider — the ACAS Code suggests at least 10 calendar days for a written offer. Shorter timescales may not be improper (Gallagher: 48 hours for a verbal offer was acceptable) but longer is safer

Not separating protected conversation records from open HR files — if a protected conversation fails and the employer proceeds with a formal process, the formal process must stand on its own merits. Protected conversation notes contaminating the formal file undermines both the protection and the process

Not knowing how SECRET RECORDINGS interact with s.111A — if an employee secretly records a s.111A meeting, the recording is INADMISSIBLE in ordinary unfair dismissal proceedings (s.111A applies regardless of how the conversation was captured). BUT: in discrimination, whistleblowing, or automatic unfair dismissal claims, the recording IS admissible. Employers should assume every meeting may be recorded and conduct all s.111A conversations with the same care they would use in an open meeting

parts.title: Part 2 — ACAS Early Conciliation, Financial Penalties, Whistleblowing Reform, and H&S Civil Liability

sections.title: The four remaining pillars — and how they've evolved under the ERA 2025
sections.summary: The ERRA 2013 established: (1) mandatory ACAS early conciliation before tribunal claims, (2) financial penalties for employers with aggravating features, (3) the public interest test for whistleblowing, and (4) the controversial removal of employer civil liability for breach of H&S statutory duty. Each has evolved differently under subsequent legislation.
sections.text:
PILLAR 2 — MANDATORY ACAS EARLY CONCILIATION (ss.7-8):

Original: From 6 April 2014, a prospective claimant MUST contact ACAS before issuing a tribunal claim. ACAS offers conciliation for a prescribed period. If settlement is not reached, ACAS issues an early conciliation certificate. The claim cannot be submitted without this certificate. Time limits are adjusted to exclude the ACAS period.

Evolution:
— Originally a 1-month conciliation period.
— Extended to 6 weeks, then back to variable periods depending on claim type.
— December 2025: ACAS conciliation period extended to 12 WEEKS to manage rising caseloads.
— ERA 2025: Tribunal time limits extended from 3 to 6 months (October 2026) — this interacts with ACAS early conciliation by giving claimants more time overall.
— The ACAS early conciliation framework established by ERRA 2013 REMAINS the governing structure. The ERA 2025 works WITHIN it.

Client relevance: EVERY employer facing a potential tribunal claim will first receive an ACAS early conciliation notification. This is the employer's BEST opportunity to resolve the dispute before it escalates. Employers should engage with ACAS conciliation, not ignore it.

PILLAR 3 — FINANCIAL PENALTIES FOR EMPLOYERS (s.16, inserting ETA s.12A):

Original: Where an employer is found to have breached a worker's rights AND the breach has 'aggravating features', the tribunal can impose a penalty of 50% of the award, capped at £5,000, payable to the Secretary of State.

Aggravating features include: deliberate breach, malice, employer with dedicated HR resources who should have known better, repeated breaches of the same right.

Evolution:
— Penalty cap increased to £20,000 (from 6 April 2019).
— ERA 2025: Expected further increase. The financial penalty represents a shift from purely compensatory awards to PUNITIVE enforcement.
— The Fair Work Agency (from April 2026) will have its own penalty powers, operating alongside the tribunal penalty.

Client relevance: An employer who loses a tribunal case AND has aggravated the breach faces BOTH a compensation award to the employee AND a financial penalty to the state. This doubles the financial exposure.

PILLAR 4 — WHISTLEBLOWING REFORM (ss.17-20):

Original (ERRA 2013 changes to ERA 1996 Part 4A):
(a) PUBLIC INTEREST TEST: A disclosure is only protected if the worker reasonably believes it is made 'in the public interest'. This closed the Parkins v Sodexho loophole where purely personal grievances (e.g., incorrect holiday pay) could be dressed up as whistleblowing.
(b) GOOD FAITH REMOVED: The 'good faith' requirement for a qualifying disclosure was removed — but if a disclosure is not made in good faith, the tribunal can reduce the award by up to 25%.
(c) VICARIOUS LIABILITY: The employer is vicariously liable for detriment caused to a whistleblower by a CO-WORKER.
(d) EXTENDED SCOPE: Whistleblowing protection extended to healthcare professionals (GPs, nurses, etc.).

Leading case — Chesterton Global v Nurmohamed [2017] EWCA Civ 979: The public interest test has a LOW threshold. A disclosure affecting approximately 100 workers satisfies it. The worker need only have a REASONABLE BELIEF that the disclosure is in the public interest — they do not need to prove it actually is.

Evolution under ERA 2025:
— From April 2026: SEXUAL HARASSMENT becomes a qualifying disclosure under whistleblowing law. A worker who reports sexual harassment is protected from detriment and unfair dismissal as a whistleblower.
— The whistleblowing framework established by PIDA 1998 (as amended by ERRA 2013) continues as the governing structure.

PILLAR 5 — H&S CIVIL LIABILITY REPEAL (s.69):

Original (ERRA 2013 s.69, from 1 October 2013): REPEALED the right of employees to bring civil claims for breach of H&S statutory duty (i.e., breach of specific H&S regulations such as MHSWR, COSHH, Workplace Regulations). Before this change, if an employer breached a specific H&S regulation and an employee was injured, the employee could sue on the basis of the statutory breach alone (strict liability). After the change, the employee must prove NEGLIGENCE — that the employer owed a duty of care, breached it, and caused the injury.

This was CONTROVERSIAL. Trade unions argued it weakened worker protections. The Government argued that negligence law provides adequate protection. The debate continues.

Evolution: The ERA 2025 does NOT reverse this change. The removal of H&S civil liability for breach of statutory duty REMAINS in force. Employees injured by H&S breaches must rely on negligence claims.

Client relevance: This matters for employers because the legal risk from H&S breaches is now DIFFERENT — not lower. The employer still owes a common law duty of care, and H&S regulatory breaches are powerful EVIDENCE of negligence even if they no longer create strict liability.

WHISTLEBLOWING — WHEN DOES A 'MINOR' ISSUE QUALIFY?

A common question for Eileen: 'An employee reported something minor — a broken handrail, a missing fire extinguisher, a leaking pipe. Is this really whistleblowing?'

The answer is almost certainly YES. Under ERA 1996 s.43B, a qualifying disclosure includes information the worker reasonably believes shows:
(a) A failure to comply with a LEGAL OBLIGATION (H&S regulations require safe premises), OR
(b) That the HEALTH OR SAFETY of any individual is being endangered.

A broken handrail IS a H&S risk. A missing fire extinguisher IS a failure to comply with fire safety regulations. The ERRA 2013 public interest test is satisfied because OTHER workers and visitors are also at risk — this is not a purely personal grievance.

The critical message for employers: treat ALL health and safety reports as potential protected disclosures. An employer who dismisses an employee for 'making a fuss about a broken handrail' may face an automatic unfair dismissal claim (no qualifying period, uncapped compensation) AND a whistleblowing detriment claim. The cost of fixing the handrail is trivial compared to the cost of the litigation.

This applies equally to reports about: missing PPE, inadequate training, understaffing creating safety risks, faulty equipment, cleaning chemical exposure, inadequate ventilation, and any other matter that engages H&S obligations. The threshold is LOW — the worker need only have a REASONABLE BELIEF that a legal obligation is being breached or safety is endangered.
sections.keyPrinciple: The ERRA 2013 reshaped the employment dispute landscape. Protected conversations gave employers a tool for managed exits. ACAS early conciliation gave both sides a structured opportunity to settle before litigation. Financial penalties added a punitive dimension for egregious breaches. The whistleblowing public interest test refocused protections on genuine concerns rather than personal grievances. The ERA 2025 builds on each of these pillars — extending time limits, increasing penalties, adding sexual harassment as a qualifying disclosure — without replacing the ERRA 2013 framework.
refs: ETA 1996 ss.18A-18B (ACAS early conciliation — inserted by ERRA 2013)
ETA 1996 s.12A (financial penalty — inserted by ERRA 2013)
Chesterton Global v Nurmohamed [2017] EWCA Civ 979
ERA 2025 — time limits, penalties, sexual harassment whistleblowing
commonErrors: Ignoring ACAS early conciliation — the employer who ignores an ACAS notification misses the best opportunity to resolve the dispute before it reaches the tribunal. Engaging with ACAS conciliation is almost always in the employer's interest

Not understanding the financial penalty mechanism — the penalty is SEPARATE from the compensation award. It is payable to the state, not the employee. An employer who loses a case with aggravating features pays BOTH the compensation AND the penalty

Treating all employee complaints as whistleblowing — the ERRA 2013 public interest test means that purely personal grievances do NOT qualify. But the threshold is LOW (Chesterton v Nurmohamed) — if the complaint affects other workers, it likely qualifies

Assuming H&S regulatory breaches no longer have legal consequences — the ERRA 2013 removed STRICT LIABILITY for breach of statutory duty, but negligence claims remain. A breach of H&S regulations is powerful evidence of negligence and can still result in substantial damages

Not understanding that sexual harassment becomes a qualifying disclosure from April 2026 — this ERA 2025 change means that employees who report sexual harassment will have DUAL protection: under the EqA 2010 (discrimination) AND under ERA 1996 Part 4A (whistleblowing)

versions.text: IN FORCE: protected conversations (s.111A), ACAS early conciliation (mandatory from April 2014), financial penalties (from April 2014, cap raised to £20,000 from April 2019), whistleblowing public interest test (from June 2013), H&S civil liability removed (from October 2013). ERA 2025 builds on but does not replace this framework.
versions.amendedBy: ERRA 2013 as enacted, subsequently amended by ERA 2025
versions.policyRationale: The Coalition Government sought to reduce the tribunal caseload, encourage early settlement, and rebalance the employer-employee relationship. Protected conversations and ACAS early conciliation succeeded in reducing tribunal claims. The H&S civil liability change remains controversial.

pendingAmendments.instrument: ERA 2025 — tribunal time limits extended to 6 months (October 2026)
pendingAmendments.status: Interacts with ACAS early conciliation. Claimants will have 6 months (not 3) to bring claims, giving more time for ACAS conciliation and settlement discussions.
pendingAmendments.aceiImpact: HIGH — employers face longer exposure window

pendingAmendments.instrument: ERA 2025 — sexual harassment as qualifying disclosure (April 2026)
pendingAmendments.status: From April 2026, sexual harassment becomes a qualifying disclosure under whistleblowing law. Workers reporting sexual harassment will be protected under both the EqA 2010 and ERA 1996 Part 4A.
pendingAmendments.aceiImpact: HIGH — dual protection pathway for sexual harassment reports

pendingAmendments.status: The ERRA 2013 formula (lower of statutory cap or one year's gross pay) will be SUPERSEDED by the removal of the cap entirely. Unfair dismissal compensation will be uncapped from January 2027.
pendingAmendments.aceiImpact: VERY HIGH — fundamentally changes the financial risk of unfair dismissal for high earners

surveillanceStatus.status: IN FORCE — ERA 2025 enhancements commencing in phases
